title: Reflecting on Action Outcomes
concept: Prompting an agent to evaluate its own tool results: 'Did this action work? Did it return the expected data?'.

exercises.prompt: Welcome to Self-Reflection & Critique Loops! Quick thought: what does it mean for an AI agent to 'take an action'?

options.text: It uses a tool, like searching the web or calling an app, to get something done
It shuts itself down to save power
It waits silently until a human types again

exercises.explain: An agent takes an action by USING a tool, like searching, calculating, or fetching data. This unit is about the next step: checking whether that action actually worked.

cards.title: Wait... Did That Even Work?
cards.body: Imagine you send a robot to grab milk, but it comes back with an empty bag. A smart agent doesn't just move on. It pauses and asks: 'Did my action actually give me what I needed?'

cards.title: Reflecting on Outcomes
cards.body: This means asking an AI agent to check its own tool results. After it runs an action, it evaluates: 'Did this work? Did it return the data I expected?' It's like proofreading your own homework.

cards.title: Tasting While You Cook
cards.body: A good chef tastes the soup before serving it. If it's too salty, they fix it. An agent that reflects 'tastes' its results and catches mistakes before handing them to you.

cards.title: The Weather Check
cards.body: An agent calls a weather tool for 'Tokyo tomorrow' but gets back an error message. Reflecting, it thinks: 'That's not weather data, that's an error. My action failed, let me try again.'

cards.title: Myth: Tools Always Work
cards.body: Beginners assume every tool call succeeds. Actually, tools can fail, time out, or return wrong data. That's exactly why reflecting on the outcome matters.

cards.title: Blind vs. Reflective
cards.body: One agent trusts every result. The other checks first. Guess which one makes fewer mistakes?

left.text: Takes the tool result and uses it, even if it's an error or empty.

right.text: Asks 'Did this work? Is this the data I expected?' before moving on.

exercises.prompt: An agent searches for a customer's order number but the tool returns 'No results found.' What should a reflective agent do?

options.text: Notice the action failed and try a different search or ask for clarification
Report to the user that the order was successfully found
Pretend the empty result is the order details
Ignore the result and repeat the exact same search forever

exercises.explain: 'No results found' means the action didn't return the expected data. A reflective agent recognizes this and adjusts, instead of pretending it worked.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Reflecting on an outcome means checking whether a tool actually returned the data you expected.
exercises.explain: That's exactly it. Reflection is the agent double-checking its own tool result to confirm the action worked and gave useful data.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about what a reflective agent does after an action.
exercises.sentence: After running a tool, the agent should evaluate whether the action returned the expected data.
exercises.explain: To reflect means to evaluate the result, checking if the action worked and gave the right data before moving on.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is an AI-generated self-reflection prompt for an agent?

options.text: Review the tool's output above. Did the action succeed, and does the returned data match what was requested? If not, explain what went wrong.
Just give the user whatever comes back, no need to look at it.
Turn off the tool and end the conversation immediately.

exercises.explain: The first is a real reflection prompt: it tells the agent to review its output and judge whether the action worked, which is the heart of this lesson.

exercises.prompt: An agent is asked to fetch a user's account balance. The tool returns a blank response. The agent then tells the user 'Your balance is: '. What went wrong, and what should a reflective agent have done?

options.text: The agent skipped reflection; it should have noticed the blank result, recognized the action didn't return expected data, and retried or reported the problem
Nothing went wrong; a blank balance is a valid answer to share
The agent should never use tools at all to avoid errors
The agent should have made up a random balance number instead

exercises.explain: The agent trusted a broken result. Reflecting means asking 'Did this action work? Did it return the expected data?' A blank response clearly fails that check, so the agent should retry or flag the issue, never pass along or invent data. That self-check is what makes an agent reliable.
